g.data-narration: This topic covers Detailed Design in Software Engineering, including key concepts like UML diagrams, design patterns, practical examples, and implementation strategies for software systems.
g.text: Detailed Design in Software Engineering

g.data-narration: Detailed Design translates system architecture into implementable components, focusing on module internals, data structures, algorithms, and interface specifications to ensure code readiness for developers.
g.text: What is Detailed Design?
Detailed Design is the phase where system architecture is translated into implementable components.
It focuses on module internals, data structures, algorithms, and interface specifications.
This phase bridges the gap between high-level architecture and actual code implementation.

g.data-narration: Key concepts include Design Patterns like Singleton, Factory, Observer and UML diagrams for class, sequence, and activity modeling with practical data flow design techniques.
g.text: Key Concepts
Design Patterns: Singleton, Factory, Observer | UML: Class, Sequence, Activity Diagrams
Data Flow Design | ER Modeling | State Transition Diagrams
Interface Design | Algorithm Specification | Coupling & Cohesion

g.data-narration: Example 1 shows a Login Module with User class containing username and password hash, and AuthManager handling authentication and logging attempts securely.
g.text: Example 1: Login Module Design
User (username, password_hash) | AuthManager (authenticate(), log_attempt())
Security: bcrypt hashing, account lockout after 3 failed attempts
Sequence: User → UI → AuthManager → Database (verify hash)

g.data-narration: Example 2 demonstrates an E-commerce checkout process with states from Cart through Shipping, Payment, to Confirmation with pricing algorithm for tax and shipping calculations.
g.text: Example 2: E-commerce Checkout Process
States: Cart → Shipping → Payment → Confirmation
Algorithm: subtotal + tax (18%) + shipping (free over 1000)
Exception Handling: Payment gateway timeout retry logic

g.data-narration: Example 3 covers Library Management with Book, Member, and Loan entities in one-to-many relationship, enforcing maximum 5 books per member with overdue fine calculations.
g.text: Example 3: Library Management ER Diagram
Entities: Book (ISBN, title), Member (ID, name), Loan (issue_date, due_date)
Relationships: Member borrows Book (1:N), Loan tracks borrowing details
Constraints: Max 5 books per member, overdue fines calculation

g.data-narration: Exam tips focus on UML conventions, SOLID principles, database normalization up to 3NF, coupling types, and validating detailed designs against functional requirements for testing.
g.text: Exam Tips
Focus on UML diagram conventions: aggregation vs composition, visibility symbols
Practice converting use cases to sequence diagrams
Memorize SOLID principles, understand normalization (1NF to 3NF)
Study coupling types: Content < Common < Control < Stamp < Data
